surface.name: Quickstart: Get started with Azure developer tools
surface.selection_basis: Microsoft's official Introduction to Azure for developers has one item under Quickstart: Get started with Azure dev tools. That guide explicitly uses the core Azure developer tools and states what the developer will have hands-on experience with by the end.

alternatives_considered.name: Azure Quickstart Center
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Microsoft describes this as the fastest environment-setup route for organizations and provides separate starter, project, and course sections; it is not the developer documentation's single quickstart.

alternatives_considered.name: Create a VM in the Azure portal
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is the Azure portal documentation's product-specific VM quickstart rather than the Azure developer introduction's general tool route.

alternatives_considered.name: Azure CLI getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is specific to command-line resource management rather than the developer introduction's three-tool quickstart.

entry_point.developer_intent: Use Microsoft's general Azure developer onboarding to deploy and inspect a sample application with the core developer tools.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Azure.
The developer has not deployed the quickstart's sample application.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Complete Quickstart: Get started with Azure developer tools and gain the hands-on experience that Microsoft lists for the end of the guide.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: A full-stack to-do application is deployed and verified, its Azure resources and logs are inspected, and GitHub Copilot for Azure returns guidance about those resources.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The deployed URL shows a to-do application where tasks can be added and completed; the created App Service and Cosmos DB resources appear in the Azure view; adding a task produces live log entries; Copilot returns step-by-step guidance tailored to the application.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The developer introduction links one Quickstart. That guide states what the developer has hands-on experience with by the end, then demonstrates deployment and browser verification, resource and log inspection, and finally a Copilot prompt that returns tailored guidance before Next steps.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: It is the terminal demonstrated state of the only quickstart in Microsoft's Azure developer introduction. The record includes the full guide through its final Copilot exercise and stops before Next steps and cleanup.

prerequisites.requirement: An Azure account with an active subscription.
A GitHub Copilot subscription is required for the GitHub Copilot for Azure portion of the terminal quickstart.
Use either the browser-based VS Code for the Web route or a local environment with Visual Studio Code and Git.

primary_path.action: Open Introduction to Azure for developers and select Get started with Azure dev tools under Quickstart.
details: The developer introduction presents this as its single quickstart.

primary_path.action: Choose VS Code for the Web or a local tool installation.
details: Microsoft calls the browser route the fastest way to get started; the local route provides a local development environment.
primary_path.success_signal: The required tool environment is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Local setup additionally requires Visual Studio Code, Git, Azure Developer CLI, the Azure Tools extension pack, and GitHub Copilot for Azure.

primary_path.action: For the browser route, open vscode.dev/azure and sign in with the Azure account.
details: The browser environment has CLIs and several extensions preinstalled.
primary_path.output: Authenticated VS Code for the Web session
primary_path.success_signal: The browser IDE opens under the signed-in account.

primary_path.action: Install the Azure Tools extension pack if all Azure tools are not already present.
details: Open Extensions, search for Azure Tools, and select Install.
primary_path.success_signal: Azure Tools is installed and accessible.

primary_path.action: Open a terminal, create a new directory, and change into it.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Directory creation fails without filesystem permission.

primary_path.action: Initialize the todo-nodejs-mongo starter template with Azure Developer CLI.
details: Enter an environment name when prompted.
primary_path.output: Full-stack Node.js, Express, MongoDB project with ARM infrastructure template
primary_path.success_signal: The project is initialized with the selected environment name.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Git is required by azd to clone the template.

primary_path.action: Run azd auth login and complete Azure sign-in.
Run azd up, then select an Azure subscription and region when prompted.
details: The command creates a resource group, provisions App Service and Azure Cosmos DB, and deploys the application code.
primary_path.output: Provisioned Azure resources and deployed application
primary_path.success_signal: Azure Developer CLI finishes and displays the deployed application URL.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Microsoft states that this process takes a few minutes.

primary_path.action: Open the displayed URL in a browser and verify the application is running.
details: The page must show a to-do application where tasks can be added and completed.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer can see the app and add or complete tasks.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missing or non-running page fails the guide's explicit verification.

primary_path.action: Run azd auth status and sign in through VS Code if account details are not returned.
details: The fallback uses Command Palette, Azure: Sign In, and browser sign-in.
primary_path.success_signal: azd auth status returns account details or VS Code sign-in completes.

primary_path.action: Open the Azure view, group resources by Resource Group, expand the subscription, and find the resource group created by azd.
details: The resource-group name starts with the environment name entered during initialization.
primary_path.success_signal: The created resource group is visible under the subscription.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect subscription or grouping can hide the expected resources.

primary_path.action: Expand the resource group and inspect the App Service and Cosmos DB resources.
details: Browse Website opens the App Service application; Open in Portal opens the Cosmos DB account.
primary_path.success_signal: The App Service, Cosmos DB account, and other created resources are shown.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing resources indicate incomplete provisioning or the wrong resource group.

primary_path.action: Start streaming logs for App Service, add a task in the to-do app, and observe the resulting log entries.
primary_path.input: Running app and App Service log stream
primary_path.success_signal: Adding a task produces entries in the streaming logs.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No log entry means the final inspection signal is not demonstrated.

primary_path.action: Open Copilot Chat and ask how to add authentication to the Azure App Service.
details: Use the prompt shown in the official quickstart.
primary_path.input: How can I add authentication to my Azure App Service?
primary_path.success_signal: Copilot returns step-by-step guidance tailored to the application.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A GitHub Copilot subscription is required.

branches.condition: The developer selects local installation instead of VS Code for the Web.
branches.path: Install Azure Developer CLI, Visual Studio Code, Git, the Azure Tools extension pack, and GitHub Copilot for Azure locally, then continue in the local terminal and IDE.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds local software installation before project initialization.

branches.condition: azd auth status does not return account details.
branches.path: Open Command Palette, select Azure: Sign In, and complete browser sign-in.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an IDE authentication recovery step before resource inspection.

friction_gates.description: The developer must choose browser-based or local tooling.
An Azure account with an active subscription is required.
The Azure Tools extension pack must be available; the local branch adds Azure Developer CLI, VS Code, Git, and Copilot installation.
Azure Developer CLI authentication is required before provisioning.
The deployment requires an Azure subscription and region choice.
Microsoft says resource provisioning and deployment takes a few minutes.
A GitHub Copilot subscription is required for the terminal Copilot exercise.

sources.title: Introduction to Azure for developers
evidence_supported: Single quickstart presented in the general Azure developer introduction

sources.title: Quickstart: Get started with Azure developer tools
sections_used: Deploy a sample app with azd
Browse resources with Azure Tools for VS Code

sources.title: Get started with the Azure Quickstart Center
evidence_supported: Starter checklist, project, and training branches

uncertainties.question: Does the Azure account's active subscription incur charges for the quickstart resources?
uncertainties.impact: The deployment provisions App Service and Cosmos DB resources and may create a cost gate.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The quickstart requires an active subscription and instructs cleanup to avoid charges, but it does not state the exact cost of the bounded path.

excluded_after_success: Exploring additional Azure Developer CLI templates
Adding authentication beyond receiving Copilot guidance
Running azd down cleanup after the terminal quickstart state
